title: Federal Legislative Process: How a Bill Becomes Law

content.summary: The federal legislative process requires bicameral passage through the House and Senate followed by presidential action, with multiple procedural checkpoints that enable deliberation and amendment.
content.body: A bill's journey through Congress follows these steps: (1) Introduction: Only members of Congress may introduce bills; representatives file in the House clerk's office, senators on the Senate floor. Bills receive numbering (H.R. for House, S. for Senate). (2) Committee Assignment: The Speaker of the House or Senate presiding officer refers the bill to relevant committee(s). Approximately 90% of bills die in committee without action. (3) Committee Action: Committees may hold hearings, request executive agency input, mark up (amend) the bill, vote to report it favorably/unfavorably, or table it. (4) Floor Consideration: House bills go to the Rules Committee which sets debate terms; Senate bills go directly to the floor. House debate is limited; Senate debate is generally unlimited unless cloture invoked. (5) Voting: Simple majority required in both chambers, though budget reconciliation requires only 51 votes in Senate. (6) Conference Committee: If chambers pass different versions, a conference committee reconciles differences; both chambers must approve the conference report. (7) Presidential Action: President may sign (becomes law), veto (returns to Congress; two-thirds override required in each chamber), or allow to become law without signature after 10 days (excluding Sundays). Pocket veto occurs if Congress adjourns during the 10-day period.
key_points: Only members of Congress may introduce legislation
Committee referral determines initial fate; ~90% die in committee
House Rules Committee controls floor debate terms
Senate filibuster requires 60 votes for cloture on most legislation
Identical text must pass both chambers
Presidential veto requires two-thirds supermajority to override
examples: Affordable Care Act (H.R. 3590): Passed House 219-212, Senate 60-39, signed March 2010
Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (H.R. 1): Used reconciliation procedure, passed Senate 51-48, signed December 2017
Veto override example: Justice Against Sponsors of Terrorism Act (2016), overridden 97-1 in Senate, 348-77 in House

sources.citation: Congressional Research Service. 'The Legislative Process.' RL30294.

temporal_scope.notes: Procedures evolve with each Congress through rule changes
